question.text: An inverted yield curve, where short-term rates are higher than long-term rates, is generally considered an indicator of:

explanation.text: **The Logic:** An inverted yield curve (short-term rates > long-term rates) historically signals an impending economic recession. It can occur when policy rates are kept high to control excess demand, or when markets expect future interest rates to decline as the economy weakens. **The Trap:** Rising inflation expectations would typically steepen the yield curve (higher long-term rates), not invert it. **Cross-Exam Context:** Yield curve shapes and their economic implications are tested in X-A, IV, and I. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When the yield curve inverts, advise clients to shift from cyclical stocks to defensive sectors and consider increasing allocation to high-quality long-term bonds to lock in yields.
